unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5, which is all about actions and activities — the things people do. You will learn thirteen action words, including build, discuss, protect and celebrate. You will talk about what you did using the past simple, explain reasons and results with because and so, order events with First, Next, Then and Finally, ask Wh- questions, and share opinions politely. The unit story, “The Wall Behind the Garden”, follows Amal, Leo and their class as they build a reading wall together.
unit.learningPath:
Start with Vocabulary and learn one word group each day.
Work through the six Grammar lessons, which cover all seven grammar topics from the source file.
Read the five passages in Reading and listening, finishing with the story “The Wall Behind the Garden”.
Answer the twelve comprehension questions, quoting a detail for each one.
Move through Speaking, then Writing, then Activities, in that order.
Finish with the unit quiz and the Self-Assessment.

visual.alt: Children using action words during active learning

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To talk about something with other people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will discuss our ideas in a group.
practiceSentences: Our class will discuss the plan for the reading wall.
Partners discuss their answers before telling the class.
Shall we discuss which game to play at break?
Theo said, "Let us discuss the ending of the story."
I discuss my drawings with my big sister.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - s - c - u - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult sitting beside me, ask me to discuss a class plan out loud, then tell me whether I used discuss for talking with others rather than talking alone.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To use your ears to hear carefully.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please listen to the teacher when she talks.
practiceSentences: We listen to Teacher Yasmin before we start our work.
We listen quietly when the coach gives a talk.
Do you listen for the birds in the morning?
The coach said, "Listen for the whistle, then run."
I listen carefully when Dad explains new things.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - i - s - t - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Read me three short instructions while my adult helper listens too, then check that I can repeat them in order to show I did listen.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To say kindly that you will give or do something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I will offer help if my friend falls down.
practiceSentences: Nora will offer to carry the heavy basket home.
Neighbours offer food to each other during harvest week.
Will you offer your turn to the younger boy?
Sami said, "I offer to sweep the classroom on Mondays."
I offer my umbrella when a friend gets wet.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Describe a person who needs help while my adult helper is here, and ask me to offer help in one kind sentence that begins with I will offer.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make something by putting parts together.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will build a wall with smooth stones.
practiceSentences: Our class will build a reading wall this term.
The workers build the new classroom brick by brick.
Can we build a bridge with these blocks?
Maya said, "Birds build their nests with twigs and grass."
I helped Grandpa build a small wooden shelf.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to name the parts I would put together to build a model, then check that I said build for making and not for finishing.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To finish something or make it whole.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I will complete my homework before dinner.
practiceSentences: The class hoped to complete the wall before the weekend.
Please complete the puzzle before starting a new one.
Did you complete your reading chart this week?
The teacher said, "Complete the sentences with neat handwriting."
I feel proud when I complete a hard task.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - m - p - l - e - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me what I still need to do to complete today's work, with my adult helper listening, and check that complete means finishing the whole job.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To take something away from a place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please remove your shoes before you come in.
practiceSentences: The children remove the broken fence from the garden.
Please remove the muddy boots at the door.
Can you remove the stickers without tearing the page?
Mum said, "Remove your coat and hang it up."
I remove my shoes before entering the house.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - m - o - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Name three things in my room and ask me which one I should remove, then check that my answer says where I would take it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To grow or become better over time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We develop our reading by practising every day.
practiceSentences: New ideas help a whole town develop over time.
Strong readers develop their skills by practising daily.
How do seeds develop into tall sunflowers?
The coach said, "Practice helps your muscles develop."
I develop my handwriting by copying neat letters.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - v - e - l - o - p
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me which skill I want to develop this month and how long it might take, then check that develop shows slow growth, not one quick change.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To keep someone or something safe from harm.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A helmet helps protect your head.
practiceSentences: A strong fence will protect the young plants.
Thick gloves protect your hands from the thorns.
How do umbrellas protect us from the rain?
Dad said, "Seatbelts protect everyone in the car."
I protect my little sister when we cross roads.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - o - t - e - c - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With my adult helper here, ask me to name one thing that can protect me outdoors and say what danger it keeps away.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To go from one place to another, often far away.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will travel to the village by bus.
practiceSentences: The visitor had to travel from a nearby town.
Families travel by bus to the summer fair.
How long does it take to travel to the coast?
Adam said, "One day I will travel by ship."
I travel to school on foot with my brother.
dictionaryLinks.sentenceStarter: One day I will travel to ______.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - r - a - v - e - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me where I would travel and how I would get there, then check that I named both the place and the transport.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To get away from a place or a situation.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The goat tried to escape from the pen.
practiceSentences: Goats used to escape through that old animal path.
The clever cat tried to escape through the window.
How did the chickens escape from the coop?
Theo said, "The rabbit dug a hole to escape."
I read about a fish that could escape every net.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - s - c - a - p - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell me the start of an animal story and ask me how the animal might escape, then check that my sentence says what it got away from.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Let us search for my lost pencil.
practiceSentences: First we search the garden for smooth flat stones.
The children search the shelf for the missing globe.
Shall we search under the desks for your pencil?
Maya said, "Search your bag again before you worry."
I search the sky for the first evening star.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - a - r - c - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Hide nothing real, but ask me where I would search for a missing book at home, and check that search means looking carefully.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To do something happy and special for a good time or a win.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will celebrate after the spelling contest.
practiceSentences: The whole town came to celebrate the finished wall.
Our class will celebrate finishing one hundred books.
How does your family celebrate a birthday morning?
Sami said, "We celebrate sports day with songs and games."
I celebrate every goal my team scores.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - e - l - e - b - r - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me what my class could do to celebrate finishing a project, then check that my idea sounds happy and is shared with others.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: What will happen in the story next?
practiceSentences: Something strange did happen behind the school garden.
Exciting things happen on the last day of term.
What will happen if we mix blue and yellow?
Mina said, "Funny things always happen at Grandma Hana's house."
I wonder what will happen in chapter three.
dictionaryLinks.sentenceStarter: I wonder what will happen when ______.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - a - p - p - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Read me the first half of a short event and ask me what will happen next, then check that my answer describes an event, not a feeling.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and use many action words, such as build, listen, protect and celebrate.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: All thirteen unit verbs sorted correctly in Activities 5 and used in the Speaking 4 interview.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about things you did, using the past simple (built, listened, celebrated).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct past forms in Activities 2 and three past-tense sentences in the Speaking 1 recording.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain why something happened, using because and so.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct choices in Activities 3 and one spoken reason with because in Speaking 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Put events in order, using First, Next, Then, Finally.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: The four wall-building steps ordered correctly in Activities 4 and four ordered steps in Writing 1.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask questions with what, where, who, when, why and how.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Six different Wh- questions written in Writing 6 and asked aloud in the Speaking 4 interview.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Share your ideas politely, using I think and I don't think.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Two polite opinions recorded in Speaking 2, each softened and each giving a reason.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read and understand a story about working together to solve a problem.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Correct answers to the four story questions, each quoting a detail from The Wall Behind the Garden.

readings.title: A Busy Saturday
readings.setting: A town school, its garden and the streets nearby
readings.passageScript: On Saturday, our family had a busy day at home. First, we cleaned the yard together. My brother Adam removed the old sticks. I helped build a small stone path. Then we searched the garden for ripe tomatoes. Finally, we celebrated our hard work with cold juice on the grass. It was a wonderful day!

readings.title: Helping Hands
readings.setting: A town school, its garden and the streets nearby
readings.passageScript: Nora saw Omar the shopkeeper carrying a heavy basket. “Can I offer some help?” she asked politely. Together they carried the basket to his shop. Omar smiled. “Thank you! You have a kind heart.” Nora felt happy because she had helped someone.

readings.title: Planning the Garden
readings.setting: A town school, its garden and the streets nearby
readings.passageScript: “First, we will search for good seeds. Then, we will build a small fence to protect the plants. After that, we will water the garden every day. Finally, we will celebrate when the first flower grows!”

readings.title: What Happened?
readings.setting: A town school, its garden and the streets nearby
readings.passageScript: “Yesterday the wind blew hard and the wall began to shake. We ran to hold the frame. We did not complete the support, so we will fix it today. I think we should add more stones.”

readings.title: The Wall Behind the Garden
readings.setting: A town school, its garden and the streets nearby
readings.passageScript:
It was the first week of school in a small town. The rain had stopped, and the fields were dry and warm. The children helped sweep the schoolyard and water the garden behind the classrooms.
That morning, Teacher Yasmin smiled and said, “Today we will start a special project. Together, we will build a new reading wall in the garden.”
“What will be on the wall?” asked Amal.
“Stories, poems, and pictures,” said Teacher Yasmin. “But first, we must remove the old, broken fence.”
The class went outside. Some children pulled weeds. Others removed old wires and sticks. Then they saw something strange. A hole lay under stones and dry leaves.
“What happened here?” asked Leo, looking closely.
“I do not know,” said Amal. “Let us discuss it with Teacher Yasmin.”
Teacher Yasmin looked at the hole. “Long ago this was an animal path,” she said. “Goats used to escape from this yard.”
The children laughed. “We do not want goats in our classroom!”
“Then we must block the path well,” said Teacher Yasmin. “Let us plan carefully and build a strong wall.”
First, they searched for smooth, flat stones. Then, they discussed the design and the colours. Next, they began to build.
Amal painted birds. Leo drew a blue waterfall. Some children wrote short poems.
But suddenly the clouds turned dark. The wind blew hard, and the wall began to shake.
“Hold the frame!” shouted Teacher Yasmin. Amal ran to help. Together they held the pieces until the wind slowed down.
“We need to fix it,” said Leo. “We did not complete the support.”
“I think we should add more stones,” said Amal.
“I do not think paint alone is strong enough,” said Sami.
The next day, everyone came early. “We will finish today,” said Teacher Yasmin. “Let us work together and be patient.” They completed the wall and made it much stronger.
The final touch was a sign. It read: “We built this wall to share words and protect dreams.”
Later that week, the whole town came to celebrate. There was singing, clapping, and food from every family.
“This is a true celebration,” said Teacher Yasmin. “You all helped. You worked hard.”
“Amal's drawing is my favourite,” said the head teacher.
“And Leo's poem,” said Teacher Yasmin. “He wrote about how we protect our stories and our voices.”
After the event, Amal saw a younger girl standing near the wall.
“Can I draw here one day?” the girl asked shyly.
“Of course,” said Amal kindly. “This wall is for everyone.”
That night, Amal wrote in her notebook: “We built something beautiful. It was windy and messy. But we listened to each other. We shared our ideas. We offered help. And we finished something strong.”
The next morning, a visitor came to the school. He had travelled from a nearby town.
“I work in education,” he said. “I heard you made something special.” The students proudly gave him a tour of the wall.
“You are helping this place develop,” he said. “This wall is not just a wall. It is a gift.”
When he left, Leo asked, “Why did he call it a gift?”
“Because it gives people ideas,” said Teacher Yasmin. “And new ideas are part of how a town grows and develops.”
Amal nodded and smiled. “And we made it,” she said. “Together.”

comprehension.question: In A Busy Saturday, what did Adam remove from the yard?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Adam removed the old sticks from the yard.
comprehension.explanation: The second sentence of the recount names Adam and the old sticks he took away.

comprehension.question: Which three time words show the order of the family's jobs on Saturday?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The family's jobs are ordered by First, Then and Finally.
comprehension.explanation: Each new job in the recount opens with a time word, so the reader can follow the day.

comprehension.question: What did Nora offer to do for Omar in Helping Hands?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She offered to help him carry his heavy basket.
comprehension.explanation: Nora's question, “Can I offer some help?”, comes just after she sees the heavy basket.

comprehension.question: Why did Nora feel happy at the end? Answer using because.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Nora felt happy because she had helped someone.
comprehension.explanation: The final sentence gives the reason straight after the word because.

comprehension.question: How many steps are in the garden plan, and what is step two?
comprehension.correctAnswer: There are four steps, and step two is to build a small fence.
comprehension.explanation: The speaker marks the steps with First, Then, After that and Finally.

comprehension.question: Why do the friends build a small fence around the plants?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They build the fence to protect the plants.
comprehension.explanation: The plan says the fence is there to keep the young plants safe.

comprehension.question: What happened to the wall yesterday, and what caused it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The wall began to shake because the wind blew hard.
comprehension.explanation: The speaker joins the two events in one sentence, so the wind is the cause.

comprehension.question: Which two words show that the speaker is giving a polite opinion?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The words I think show that the opinion is polite.
comprehension.explanation: Starting with I think softens the idea, so it does not sound like an order.

comprehension.question: What special project did Teacher Yasmin start in the school garden?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The class started to build a new reading wall.
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin names the project in her first speech of the story.

comprehension.question: What strange thing did the children find in the ground?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They found a hole covered by stones and dry leaves.
comprehension.explanation: The hole appears right after the children remove the wires and sticks.

comprehension.question: What did the sign on the finished wall say?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The sign said: “We built this wall to share words and protect dreams.”
comprehension.explanation: The exact words are quoted in the story, so the answer can be copied carefully.

comprehension.question: Why did the visitor call the wall a gift?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He called it a gift because it gives other people new ideas.
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin explains his meaning in the last part of the story.

grammar.title: The Past Simple Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the past simple to talk about actions that already happened and are finished. It helps us tell stories and share the things we did. In this unit's story the children built a wall, so we say built, not build.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For most verbs, add -ed: discuss becomes discussed, listen becomes listened, celebrate becomes celebrated.
Some verbs are irregular and must be learned: build becomes built, go becomes went, have becomes had.
A time word often joins the sentence: yesterday, last week, a long time ago.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Yesterday they build a house.” The action is already finished, so the present form is wrong.
Fix: “Yesterday they built a house.”
grammar.memoryTip: The past simple is a little time machine. If the job is done, take it back in time: add -ed, and remember the special ones like built and went.
grammar.practice: Write the past simple form of the verb in brackets: | Last summer they ______ (build) a reading wall. | She ______ (celebrate) her birthday with friends. | He ______ (remove) the old sign yesterday. | We ______ (listen) to the teacher this morning.

grammar.title: Sentence Starters for Explanations
grammar.explanation: Sometimes we need to say why something happened or when it happened. Starter words such as because, when, since and as do that job. They join two ideas so the sentence carries more meaning.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
because gives the reason: “I escaped because I was scared.”
when tells the time: “It happened when it was dark.”
since also gives a reason: “She was happy since she got good news.”
as shows time or reason: “They laughed as they watched the show.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I left because.” The starter word opens an idea that is never finished.
Fix: “I left because I was tired.”
grammar.memoryTip: A starter word is a door, and you must always walk through it. After because, always say the rest of the reason.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with because, when, since or as: | I ran fast ______ I wanted to catch the bus. | We stayed inside ______ it was too cold. | He was quiet ______ it was a serious moment. | It happened ______ it was dark outside.

grammar.title: Wh- Questions
grammar.explanation: Good learners ask questions. Wh- questions begin with what, where, who, when, why and how. They ask for real information instead of a simple yes or no.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What happened? asks about a thing or an event.
Where did you travel? asks about a place, and needs the helping word did.
Who offered help? asks about a person.
When did you finish? Why did the wall shake? How did you fix it?
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Where you travelled?” The helping word is missing, so the question sounds unfinished.
Fix: In past questions use did with the base verb: “Where did you travel?”
grammar.memoryTip: Wh- words want answers, and they are impatient. The Wh- word always stands at the very front of the question.
grammar.practice: Complete each question with the correct Wh- word: | ______ is your favourite colour? | ______ did you go last weekend? | ______ helped you with your project? | ______ did the wall shake, when you want a reason?

grammar.title: Cause and Effect (because / so)
grammar.explanation: We often want to show why something happens and what follows from it. The word because points back to the reason. The word so points forward to the result.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
because points to the reason: “He removed the rubbish because it smelled.”
so points to the result: “She offered help, so I listened.”
Put a comma before so when it joins two full ideas.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She was happy so she smiled.” The comma before so is missing.
Fix: “She was happy, so she smiled.”
grammar.memoryTip: Because explains, so shows. If you can swap the two halves around, you probably needed because.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with because or so: | The wall was weak, ______ we added more stones. | He was late ______ he missed the bus. | The flowers wilted ______ they did not get water. | I forgot my homework, ______ I felt sad.

grammar.title: Time Words for Sequencing
grammar.explanation: Time words tell the reader what came first, next and last. They hold a report together, just like the children who planned and built the wall one step at a time.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
First, they searched for smooth stones.
Then, they discussed the design and colours.
Next, they began to build.
Finally, they celebrated together.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “They started to build. First, they discussed the idea.” The events arrive in the wrong order.
Fix: “First, they discussed the idea. Then, they started to build.”
grammar.memoryTip: Time words are a story map: First, Next, Then, Finally. Follow the map and no reader gets lost.
grammar.practice: Put the time words in order. Fill in with First, Next, Then or Finally: | ______, they gathered their materials. | ______, they discussed the plan. | ______, they built the wall. | ______, they celebrated their success!

grammar.title: Polite Opinions and Word Families
grammar.explanation: Two friendly tools finish this unit. Polite opinion phrases let us disagree kindly, and word families let one base word grow into several new words.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Polite opinions: “I think travelling is fun.” “I don't think escaping was the best idea.” “In my opinion, reading is important.”
Word families: add -er for the person, so build becomes builder.
Add -ment for the result, so develop becomes development.
Add -tion for the process, so protect becomes protection and celebrate becomes celebration.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Travelling is boring!” and “I want to buildment a sandcastle.” One sounds unkind; the other bolts an ending onto the wrong word.
Fix: “I think travelling can be boring sometimes.” and “I want to build a sandcastle.”
grammar.memoryTip: A polite opinion is a soft cushion, and a word family is a tree: the base word is the trunk and each ending is a branch.
grammar.practice: Part A. Rewrite each strong sentence politely with I think or I don't think: | Group work is fun. | Staying up late is a good idea. | This story is exciting. | Part B. Write the correct form of the word in brackets: | The ______ (build) is making a new house. | The ______ (develop) of the garden took many weeks. | A helmet gives good ______ (protect).

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Tell What You Did
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Think of something you did yesterday or last week. Get ready to say three sentences in order:
“First, I ______.”
“Then, I ______.”
“Finally, I ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use the past simple? Did you use time words? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my three past-tense sentences with my adult helper nearby, then tell me which verb still needs its past form.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Share a Polite Opinion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose a topic (a game, a food, a story). Say what you think, politely:
“I think ______ because ______.”
“I don't think ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me for my opinion about a class game while my adult helper listens, then say whether my wording sounded kind and gave a reason.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — What Happened Next?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person starts a story: “The goat tried to escape from the pen …”
The next person adds one action: “Then it ran to the garden and ______.”
Keep taking turns, using action words, until the story is finished. Ask “What happened next?” each time.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Take turns with me on the escaped-goat story with an adult in the room, and stop me if my next event does not follow the one before.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Work with a partner while an adult is nearby. Choose six unit verbs, for example discuss, protect, travel, search, develop and celebrate.
Ask your partner one Wh- question about each verb, such as “When did you last protect something?”
Your partner answers in a full sentence that uses the verb.
Swap roles, then record your best three questions and answers.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Play the interview partner for six unit verbs with my adult helper present, and check that each of my questions starts with a Wh- word.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Explain to your listener why the reading wall began to shake in the story.
Give your reason with because, then give the result with so.
Your listener asks you two follow-up questions, such as “Why did they add more stones?”
Answer each one in a complete sentence, then record your explanation.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to explain why the wall shook, with an adult listening in, then push me for one more sentence that begins with so.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Plan a one-minute talk that retells The Wall Behind the Garden.
Open with one sentence about the project, then use First, Next, Then and Finally for the four main events.
Close with one sentence saying why the visitor called the wall a gift.
Practise twice with an adult listening, then record your best try.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Time my one-minute retelling of the wall story with my adult helper beside me, then tell me which time word I forgot to use.

writing.title: Writing 1 — How To Do Something
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four steps to do a simple activity, such as how to plant a seed or how to tidy your room. Use time words:
First, ______.
Next, ______.
Then, ______.
Finally, ______.
writing.modelText: A strong response gives four steps in the right order, each opening with its own time word and naming one real action.
writing.expectedLength: 4 numbered steps plus one closing sentence
writing.successCriteria: I used all four time words; I put the steps in the right order; each step names one action; I checked capitals and full stops.
writing.support: Say the four steps aloud in order before you write anything down.
writing.extension: Add one warning sentence that begins with Be careful when.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Something You Built or Made
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three or four sentences about a time you built, made, or fixed something. Answer these questions in your writing:
What did you build or make?
Who helped you?
What happened while you were doing it?
How did you feel when you completed it?
writing.modelText: A strong response answers all four questions and keeps every verb in the past simple, such as built, helped and felt.
writing.successCriteria: I answered all four questions; every finished action is in the past simple; I named the person who helped me.
writing.support: List the four answers as short notes, then turn each note into one sentence.
writing.extension: Add one sentence with because to explain why you felt that way.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Our Class Project Report
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence report about a project your class has done. Start with a topic sentence that names the project. Join at least two ideas with because and one with so.
writing.modelText: A strong report opens by naming the project, then explains one reason with because and one result with so.
writing.successCriteria: I began with a topic sentence; I used because once and so once; my sentences stay on the same project.
writing.support: Write your topic sentence first and read it aloud before adding the rest.
writing.extension: Add a closing sentence that says what you would change next time.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Information Text: A Reading Wall
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five true facts about how a reading wall is made. Use at least four unit verbs, such as search, build, complete and protect. Number each fact.
writing.modelText: A strong information text uses a clear heading, numbered facts, and precise verbs instead of vague words like did or got.
writing.sentenceStarter: How To Make A Reading Wall
writing.successCriteria: My heading names the topic; I wrote five facts; I used four unit verbs; each fact is one clear sentence.
writing.support: Reread Planning the Garden and borrow two of its steps as facts.
writing.extension: Add a labelled drawing that shows where the stones and the sign go.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose one piece of writing you finished earlier in this unit. Rewrite three of its sentences to make them clearer. Add two new details. Then write one sentence beginning I think, saying which change helped most.
writing.modelText: A strong reflection shows the old sentence and the new one side by side, then names the improvement politely.
writing.sentenceStarter: I think my best change was ______.
writing.expectedLength: 3 rewritten sentences, 2 new details and 1 reflection sentence
writing.successCriteria: I rewrote three sentences; I added two details; my reflection sentence begins with I think and gives a reason.
writing.support: Underline the three sentences you want to change before you rewrite them.
writing.extension: Ask a partner which change they noticed first, and write their answer down.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Six Questions for the Visitor
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: The visitor in the story said the wall was a gift. Write six questions you would ask him. Use what, where, who, when, why and how, one for each question. End every question with a question mark.
writing.modelText: A strong set of questions uses a different Wh- word each time and keeps the helping word did in past questions.
writing.sentenceStarter: Why did you call our wall ______?
writing.expectedLength: 6 questions, one for each Wh- word
writing.successCriteria: I used all six Wh- words; every question ends with a question mark; my past questions include did.
writing.support: Write the six Wh- words down the page first, then build one question beside each.
writing.extension: Write the answer you think the visitor would give to your best question.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Match the Action Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each action word to what it means. Write the letter next to the number.
1. protect (a) to look for something
2. search (b) to finish something
3. complete (c) to keep something safe
4. celebrate (d) to do something happy for a special time

activities.title: Activities 2 — Past Simple
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the correct past simple form of the verb in brackets.
1. Yesterday we ______ (build) a stone path.
2. She ______ (celebrate) her birthday last week.
3. The children ______ (listen) to the teacher.
4. He ______ (remove) the broken fence.

activities.title: Activities 3 — because or so?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill in each blank with because or so.
1. The wall was weak, ______ we added more stones.
2. I wore a helmet ______ I wanted to protect my head.
3. It started to rain, ______ we went inside.
4. She smiled ______ she was happy.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Put the Steps in Order
activities.instructionsAndItems:
These sentences about building the wall are mixed up. Write them in the correct order using First, Then, Next, Finally.
They celebrated together.
They searched for smooth stones.
They discussed the design.
They began to build.
activities.answerSummary: First, they searched for smooth stones. Then, they discussed the design. Next, they began to build. Finally, they celebrated together.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Sort all thirteen unit verbs into the four word groups, then write one sentence explaining the rule for each group.
Verbs: discuss, listen, offer, build, complete, remove, develop, protect, travel, escape, search, celebrate, happen.
Groups: Working Together, Getting Things Done, Going and Finding, Special Times.
activities.answerSummary: Working Together: discuss, listen, offer, build. Getting Things Done: complete, remove, develop, protect. Going and Finding: travel, escape, search. Special Times: celebrate, happen.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six details in the unit texts, at least one from each of the five passages. Copy each detail exactly, name the passage it came from, and write one sentence saying what it tells the reader.
activities.answerSummary: Six exact quotations, each labelled with its passage and followed by one sentence of explanation.

assignments.title: Actions and Activities learning portfolio
assignments.instructions:
Submit three pieces: Writing 1 — How To Do Something, Writing 6 — Six Questions for the Visitor, and a recording of Speaking 1 — Tell What You Did. Improve each piece once using the Grade 3 rubrics before you hand it in.
Marking guidance for the teacher: award up to 16 marks with the writing rubric and up to 16 with the speaking rubric. In Writing 1, look for four steps in order, each opening with First, Next, Then or Finally. In Writing 6, look for all six Wh- words, a question mark on every line, and did in the past questions. In the Speaking 1 recording, look for three sentences in sequence with every finished action in the past simple, and for clear, unhurried delivery.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does the word protect mean in this unit?
quizzes.options: To look carefully for something you have lost. | To finish a job so that nothing is left over. | To keep someone or something safe from harm. | To go from one place to another far away.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To keep someone or something safe from harm.
quizzes.explanation: Protect is about safety, so the helmet in the example sentence keeps your head from harm.

quizzes.question: In The Wall Behind the Garden, what did the class remove before they could build?
quizzes.options: The old, broken fence. | The stone reading wall. | The tomatoes in the garden. | The sign about sharing words.
quizzes.explanation: Teacher Yasmin says the broken fence must go first, before any building starts.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the past simple correctly?
quizzes.options: Yesterday they build a house. | Yesterday they builds a house. | Yesterday they are build a house. | Yesterday they built a house.
quizzes.explanation: The time word yesterday shows the action is finished, and the past form of build is built.

quizzes.question: Choose the word that fits: The wall was weak, ______ we added more stones.
quizzes.explanation: Adding stones is the result of the weak wall, and a result takes so after the comma.

quizzes.question: In Helping Hands, what did Nora offer to do?
quizzes.options: Carry the heavy basket. | Sweep the classroom floor. | Search for a lost pencil. | Paint some birds on the wall.
quizzes.explanation: Nora asks her question the moment she sees Omar struggling with the basket.

quizzes.question: Which verb means to grow or become better over time?
quizzes.explanation: Develop describes slow change, which is why a town or a skill can develop.

quizzes.question: In Planning the Garden, why do the friends build a small fence?
quizzes.options: To search for good seeds. | To protect the plants. | To celebrate the first flower. | To water the garden every day.
quizzes.explanation: The plan states the purpose of the fence straight after the word to.

quizzes.question: Which question about a past journey is written correctly?
quizzes.options: Where you travelled last week? | Where travelled you last week? | Where did you travelled last week? | Where did you travel last week?
quizzes.correctAnswer: Where did you travel last week?
quizzes.explanation: Past questions need the helping word did, and after did the verb goes back to its base form.

quizzes.question: Which of these things did the class do first when they made the wall?
quizzes.options: They searched for smooth stones. | They celebrated with the town. | They discussed the design and colours. | They began to build the wall.
quizzes.explanation: The story marks this step with First, and the other three follow it with Then, Next and later.

quizzes.question: Which sentence shares an opinion politely?
quizzes.options: Group work is boring! | Nobody enjoys group work. | I think group work is fun. | Group work always wastes our time.
quizzes.correctAnswer: I think group work is fun.
quizzes.explanation: The words I think turn a strong statement into a personal opinion others can disagree with kindly.

liveSessions.title: Actions and Activities: Launch, vocabulary and learning goals
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the unit overview and learn the four verbs in Working Together.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min unit launch and word wall; 10 min teacher models discuss, listen, offer and build; 15 min group sort of the thirteen verbs; 10 min partner sentences using two verbs each; 5 min goal setting for the fortnight
liveSessions.afterSession: Add the four Working Together verbs to your word wall and tick the ones you can already use.

liveSessions.title: Actions and Activities: Reading fluency and comprehension
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read A Busy Saturday and Helping Hands once each, out loud.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min recap of the verb groups; 10 min shared reading of Helping Hands with expression; 15 min guided practice finding evidence in both recounts; 10 min partner answers to two comprehension questions; 5 min feedback on quoting exactly
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer comprehension questions one to four and underline the words that prove each answer.

liveSessions.title: Actions and Activities: Grammar workshop on the past simple
liveSessions.beforeSession: Work through Grammar lessons 1 and 2, then try their practice items.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min retrieval of yesterday's verbs; 10 min teacher models -ed and the irregular built and went; 15 min guided workshop on Activities 2; 10 min partner correction of four wrong sentences; 5 min feedback on time words
liveSessions.afterSession: Rewrite four of your own sentences in the past simple and bring them to the next session.

liveSessions.title: Actions and Activities: Wh- questions, dialogue and pronunciation
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare six Wh- questions about the reading wall project.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min question warm-up round; 10 min teacher models did in past questions; 15 min paired Vocabulary Expert Interview; 10 min pronunciation drill on develop, celebrate and protect; 5 min reflection on turn taking
liveSessions.afterSession: Record the Speaking 4 interview and note one question you would improve.

liveSessions.title: Actions and Activities: Writing conference and revision
liveSessions.beforeSession: Draft Writing 1 and bring it with the four time words marked.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min success criteria review; 10 min teacher models revising a weak step; 15 min individual conferences on Writing 1; 10 min partner check for capitals and full stops; 5 min setting one revision target each
liveSessions.afterSession: Revise Writing 1, then complete Writing 5 by explaining the change that helped most.

liveSessions.title: Actions and Activities: Story performance, assessment and reflection
liveSessions.beforeSession: Reread The Wall Behind the Garden and choose a part to read aloud.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min warm-up retelling in pairs; 10 min modelled expressive reading of the storm paragraph; 15 min one-minute unit talks in small groups; 10 min unit quiz; 5 min self-assessment and next-unit preview
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the unit quiz and the self-assessment, then look ahead to Unit 6.

teacherNotes.note: Model each new verb aloud and check meaning before independent work, then give feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and the past simple. An adult must stay present whenever a child uses the AI tutor or a recording device; no child works alone with the device, and recordings are reviewed by the adult before they are shared. Offer the verb sort on cards for learners who need a hands-on route into Activities 5.

Ask Your AI Tutor — reading and listening:
“Can you read the Planning the Garden text with me and explain it?”
“Can you tell me a plan and let me put the steps in order?”
Ask Your AI Tutor — speaking:
“Can you help me practise telling what I did yesterday?”
“Can you play What Happened Next with me?”
“Can you check whether my opinion sounds polite?”
Ask Your AI Tutor — writing:
“Can you check whether I used the past simple correctly?”
“Can you help me put my steps in the right order?”
Ask Your AI Tutor — vocabulary:
“Can you quiz me on the four Working Together verbs?”
“Can you give me another sentence using the word offer?”
“What is the difference between listen and hear?”
“Can you show me the word families for build, develop and protect?”
Ask Your AI Tutor — grammar:
“Why do we say they built but they build?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise because and so?”
Every prompt above assumes an adult is sitting with the child.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1-c, 2-a, 3-b, 4-d. If a child pairs search with complete, ask them which word means you have not found it yet.
1. built, 2. celebrated, 3. listened, 4. removed. Only build is irregular; the other three simply take -ed, and celebrate and remove already end in e.
1. so, 2. because, 3. so, 4. because. Items 1 and 3 already carry a comma, which is the clue that a result follows.

First, they searched for smooth stones. Then, they discussed the design. Next, they began to build. Finally, they celebrated together. Send the child back to the story paragraph that starts with First if the order slips.

Working Together: discuss, listen, offer, build. Getting Things Done: complete, remove, develop, protect. Going and Finding: travel, escape, search. Special Times: celebrate, happen. Accept build in either of the first two groups if the child's rule sentence explains the choice.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Look for six exact quotations, each labelled with its passage, each followed by one sentence of explanation. Strong answers include the sign wording and the visitor's line about a gift; mark down any detail invented rather than copied.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Correct: to keep someone or something safe from harm. The other three options define search, complete and travel.
Correct: the old, broken fence. The wall and the sign came later, and the tomatoes belong to a different passage.
Correct: “Yesterday they built a house.” Build is irregular, so it never takes -ed and never adds -s after they.
Correct: so. The comma before the gap signals a result; because would turn the second half into a reason instead.
Correct: carry the heavy basket. Sweeping, searching and painting all happen in other unit texts, not in Helping Hands.
Correct: develop. Escape means getting away, complete means finishing, and listen means hearing carefully.
Correct: to protect the plants. Searching, watering and celebrating are the other three steps of the same plan.
Correct: “Where did you travel last week?” Once did appears, travel must not also carry the -ed ending.
Correct: they searched for smooth stones. The story orders the steps First, Then, Next, and the celebration comes a week later.
Correct: “I think group work is fun.” The other three state a strong view as though it were a fact about everybody.

selfAssessment.statement: I can use many action words in my own sentences.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about things I did, using the past simple.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain why something happened, using because and so.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can put events in order, using First, Next, Then and Finally.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask questions with what, where, who, when, why and how.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can share my ideas politely, using I think and I don't think.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
